narrative_blocks.header: Invented the first practical lawn mower (1847)
narrative_blocks.detail: In 1847, William Ketchum patented the first practical lawn mower in the United States. In 1861, he patented the Ketchum Hand Grenade — a cast iron oval head charged with gunpowder, an 8-inch wooden tail rod with four pasteboard fins, and a percussion cap nose. Made in 1, 3, and 5 lb sizes, approximately 93,200 were procured by the Union Army. The grenade was used at the Siege of Vicksburg and the Siege of Petersburg. A famous incident at Port Hudson: Confederate defenders learned to catch them in blankets and hurl them back — the grenades often exploded on return with greater force. The 1 and 3 lb sizes were preferred as they could be thrown farther.
narrative_blocks.body: Two inventions from the same man: the first practical U.S. lawn mower patent (1847) and the Ketchum Hand Grenade (U.S. Pat. #33,089, 1861), used across multiple Civil War sieges. About 93,200 were procured by the Union Army.

narrative_blocks.header: 14th Mayor of Buffalo, Millard Fillmore appointee
narrative_blocks.detail: William Ketchum served as Buffalo Village Trustee before the city's incorporation, then on the Erie County Board of Supervisors. He won election as the 14th Mayor of Buffalo on March 5, 1844 as the Whig candidate, serving until 1845 without seeking re-election. In 1851, President Millard Fillmore appointed him Collector of the Port of Buffalo. He co-founded the original Bank of Buffalo. He was a 6th-generation descendant of Rev. Thomas Hooker and a great-grandson of Rev. Jonathan Edwards through his mother Lucy Parsons.
narrative_blocks.body: Elected 14th Mayor of Buffalo as Whig candidate March 5, 1844. Appointed Collector of the Port of Buffalo by President Millard Fillmore in 1851.

narrative_blocks.header: Author: history of Buffalo and biography of Mohawk
narrative_blocks.detail: After retiring from the fur and hat business in 1857, William Ketchum devoted himself to history. In 1864 he published An Authentic and Comprehensive History of Buffalo (2 vols.), covering the city's early inhabitants including the Iroquois Six Nations and a sketch of Sir William Johnson. In 1872 he published a Memoir of Chief Joseph Brant (Thayendanegea), the celebrated Mohawk sachem and warrior. The files of the Buffalonian newspaper are archived at the Buffalo and Erie County Historical Society.
narrative_blocks.body: Wrote a 2-volume history of early Buffalo (1864) and a memoir of Mohawk chief Captain Joseph Brant (1872).
